comments.text: Using Claude Opus 4.6 (Anthropic), I obtained a partial result on the Erdős–Herzog–Piranian conjecture ($\rho_n \ge c/n$ for monic degree-$n$ polynomials with roots in the closed unit disc). Main result. Call $P$ non-degenerate if every critical value has modulus $\ge 1$. Equivalently, $P$ has no branch points over $\mathbb{D}$ , so every inverse branch $\psi_j$ with $\psi_j(0) = z_j$ extends univalently across all of $\mathbb{D}$, with $\psi_j(\mathbb{D}) \subset \Lambda_P(1)$. Theorem. If $P$ is non-degenerate with roots in the closed unit disc, then $\operatorname{inrad}(\Lambda_P(1)) \ge \frac{1}{4n}$. Proof sketch. The Koebe quarter theorem gives $\Lambda_P(1) \supset B\left(z_j, \frac{1}{4|P'(z_j)|}\right)$ for each root. The Fekete energy of $n$ points in the closed disc is maximized by $n$-th roots of unity, giving $$\sum_j \log |P'(z_j)| \le n \log n,$$so by pigeonhole $$\min_j |P'(z_j)| \le n.$$Combining: $\operatorname{inrad} \ge \frac{1}{4n}$. The conjectured extremizer $z^n − 1$ is non-degenerate with $|P'(\omega_k)| = n$ for each root. Reduction. The full EHP conjecture reduces to: the inradius minimizer is non-degenerate. For degenerate components containing $k$ roots, the Riemann map $\varphi : \mathbb{D} \rightarrow \mathcal{C}$ gives a degree-$k$ Blaschke product $B = P \circ \varphi$, and the inradius bound becomes $\operatorname{inrad}(C) ≥ \frac{|B'(0)|}{4|P'(z^*)|}$. For all $k \ge 2$, the hyperbolic Blaschke invariants degenerate as the component approaches a pinch point — for $k = 2$, $$|B''(wc)|(1−|wc|^2)^2 = 2(1−|B(wc)|²) \rightarrow 0$$as the critical value approaches the boundary; for $k \ge 3$, the family $B_a(w) = w^2 \cdot \frac{w−a}{1−\overline{a}w}$ with $a \rightarrow 0$ shows the same degeneration at all critical points simultaneously. This creates a uniform structural obstruction for the conformal approach in the entire degenerate case. Numerical evidence. Over several hundred random configurations with $n = 10$ (roots on $\mathbb{T}$, in $\mathbb{D}$, clustered, adversarial), the minimum of $n \cdot \operatorname{inrad}$ was always attained by roots of unity. Degenerate configurations consistently had larger inradii, typically $n \cdot \rho \ge 4$. The approach is entirely conformal — no area or perimeter estimates — and is independent of KLR (2025). A literature search (via deep research) found no prior work establishing these results or using these methods. Paper: https://github.com/huy209vn/INRADIUS-OF-POLYNOMIAL-LEMNISCATES-THE-NON-DEGENERATE-CASE-AND-BEYOND Lean formalization is in progress.

Thanks for the work. Standard check claims several major gaps (incorrect arguments) or omissions (missing arguments). However, it is plausible that these can be fixed in a way that the main result stays intact. This is an AI-based check, currently by GPT-5.4 Thinking, which has been successful in finding errors in the past. Note that it is not meant to be comprehensive, and its claims of errors are not guaranteed to be correct either. I would hold off on accepting this until you have the formalization (which you're working on), or if you have a revision which seems to have sufficiently few errors.

So uh, I revised the paper quite many times with multiple claude and perplexity sessions, Claude evaluated the peer review (including natso's check), and revised the paper, reviewing by himself too. Main theorem is unchanged. The paper should have sufficiently few errors now. And on Lean formalization, Aristotle made a compiling file. | Specifically, Aristotle proved the main theorem (Theorem 2.1) in Lean 4 / Mathlib4 with four classical results axiomatized (Koebe 1/4, Fekete bound, univalent branch existence, minimizer existence). Currently running Aristotle again to deaxiomatize the Fekete bound (proving it from a Hadamard inequality axiom + Matrix.det_vandermonde from Mathlib) and extend the formalization to cover more of the paper. Updated paper + Lean code: https://github.com/huy209vn/INRADIUS-OF-POLYNOMIAL-LEMNISCATES-THE-NON-DEGENERATE-CASE-AND-BEYOND

thanks for showing the revised version. I checked it with two AIs. Gemini 3.1 Pro is in general happy, has only minor "complaints": https://gemini.google.com/share/3f0df25f4de8 In contrast, GPT 5.4 sees two major obstacles. It says * the proof of Lemma 5.1 has a real gap. * the central obstruction example for k >= 3 does not fit with the squarefree frame. (I send you link for this comment in PM.)

Thanks old-bielefelder, both catches were correct. Fixed Lemma 5.1: the old simple connectivity argument broke because a bounded complementary component can contain other lemniscate components with roots, so log|P| isn't harmonic there. Replaced with the subharmonicity argument — if γ ⊂ U bounds a domain D_γ, then |P| < 1 on γ and |P| is subharmonic, so maximum principle gives |P| < 1 on D_γ, done. Fixed the k ≥ 3 example: B_a(w) = w²(w−a)/(1−aw) has a double zero at 0, violating the squarefree hypothesis. Replaced with B_a(w) = w · (w−a)/(1−aw) · (w−2a)/(1−2aw), three simple zeros, verified the critical-point invariants still degenerate as O(a) → 0. Neither fix touches the main theorem or the Lean formalization. On Lean: good news is that Claude and Aristotle managed to cover the whole paper now. no sorry's. Four classical axioms (Koebe 1/4, Hadamard's inequality, monodromy/univalent branches, minimizer existence) plus two axioms combining classical complex analysis not yet in Mathlib (lemniscate component structure, conformal factorization — both need the Riemann mapping theorem, which is on Mathlib's open wishlist as issue #6091). The main theorem, the reduction, the z^n − 1 verification, the Fekete bound (proved from Hadamard + Vandermonde), dual Fekete (from Gauss-Lucas, which is in Mathlib), P'' identity, Lemma 5.2 chain rule, Corollary 5.3, k=2 Blaschke identity, and conformal factorization bound are all machine-checked from those axioms.

I'll check the Lean. Note that it is generally *not accepted* that LLMs can catch their own errors. Recently it seems that it may be possible, but only 1) with the right models - this would be GPT-5.2 and GPT-5.4 but not Gemini 3.1 Pro or Claude Opus 4.6 in my experience; 2) with the right processes which are nontrivial. And even if 1) and 2) are satisfied, I would still put a disclaimer with caution and I'll note that even *my* own results using this process is in general not considered accepted without some amount of human checking (depending case-by-case). For many people, even that is not considered acceptable at all. But I'll accept it if the circumstances seem right. I realize that this is difficult to get right for both sides and do welcome constructive criticisms here. So I'd just be cautious in general and will get back to you on the Lean!

Formalization confirmed to be partial result by GPT-5.4 Thinking. It is conditional on 3 inputs: KoebeQuarterStatement, FeketeBoundStatement, UnivalentBranchExistenceStatement. The first two are standard facts. The third is not a single named standard fact but is believed by GPT to be easily derivable from standard facts. Main result proved is as follows: If $P$ is monic of degree $n \ge 1$; every root of $P$ lies in the closed unit disk; $P$ is non-degenerate, meaning every critical value has modulus at least $1$, then $\rho(P) \ge 1/(4n)$. Congrats!

yeah, but i find Claude opus 4.6 is more reliable to work with, way less frustrating to use than chatgpt..I was actually just trying to get claude on the AI contribution to erdos problem wiki..seeing the lack of him there..which is odd. Also more importantly— I don't have arXiv endorsement (math.CV or math.CA). If anyone here could endorse me to put this up as a preprint, I'd really appreciate it.

I would like to point out that the same combination of ideas, Koebe one quarter and Hadamard inequality applied to a Vandermonde are already in the cited preprint of Krishnapur, Lundberg, and Ramachandran. Namely, in the proof of part (b) of Proposition 18 in that preprint. While the statement of Proposition 18 part (b) is an estimate for the area of a filled lemniscate, the proof proceeds by estimating radii of inscribed disks and produces an order 1/n estimate for the inradius. Moreover, the nondegeneracy condition (that the critical points are outside the sublevel set) is equivalent to the condition in the hypothesis of Proposition 18(b) in that preprint that the sublevel set consists of n separate components. Perhaps this is an instance of AI assimilating/reciting a chain of ideas from the literature without being able to retain information about its source.

I have made a Lean 4 / Mathlib formalization attempt of the proof strategy discussed in this thread. The formalization is based on Liam Price's natural-language proof PDF and on the discussion here. I would be grateful for any corrections or comments. GitHub repository: https://github.com/KitaKen1/erdos1039-lean Lean4Web demo: open in Lean4Web AI usage disclosure: The code was created with assistance from Codex 5.5 using xhigh reasoning.
I have confirmed this is correct.

GPT-5.5 Pro claims to show $\inf_{\mathrm{deg} f=n}\rho(f)=\Theta(1/n)$ here, but does not produce an exact asymptotic constant as $n\to\infty$. EDIT: Fixed comment and added the positivity constraint on $c$.

Congrats. Standard check found 1 minor issue in the writeup. Correction on the comment: it is not shown that $\rho(f) = \Theta(1/n)$, only that $\inf_{\deg f = n} \rho(f) = \Theta(1/n)$, which is different. There are indeed other questions that can be asked such as to estimate $\sup_{\deg f = n} \rho(f)$.

I have digested this argument and can vouch for its correctness. The argument proves that for any polynomial $p(z) = \prod_{i=1}^n |z - z_i|$, $|z_i| \le 1$, with lemniscate $\Omega_p = \{ z: |p(z)| < 1 \}$, there must be a disk centered at some zero $z_i$ with radius at least $(\log 2)/n$ which is contained in $\Omega_p$. This shows $\rho(p) \ge (\log 2)/n \gg 1/n$, answering the second question. For a strategy that always produces a disk centered at some zero $z_i$, the $\log 2$ constant is sharp. An example that demonstrates the sharpness is $p(z) = z^n - 1$. The idea is a product estimate. If no such disk exists, we can find $w_j$ such that $|p(w_j)| \ge 1$ and $|w_j - z_j| < (\log 2)/n$. This implies $$ 1 \le \prod_{j=1}^n |p(w_j)| = \prod_{j=1}^n \prod_{i=1}^n |w_j - z_i|. $$ However, this product can be upper bounded with standard tools. Specifically, if $0 < c < \log 2$ is such that $|w_j - z_j| < c/n$, then this product is at most $(\exp(c) - 1)^n < 1$, which is a contradiction. With GPT-5.5 Thinking, I have also produced a streamlined version of the argument, which can be found in these notes.

Thanks! The estimate $\rho(f)\gg 1/n$ follows immediately from the inequality that if $z_1,\ldots,z_n$ are as in the problem statement and $w_1,\ldots,w_k$ are such that $\lvert z_i-w_i\rvert\leq \epsilon$ for all $i$ then\[\prod_{j}\lvert f(w_j)\rvert \leq ((1+\epsilon)^n-1)^n.\](This is slightly different from the inequality in your note, but suffices, and is simpler to state.) I was sure that this elegant inequality must have appeared in the literature before, so asked GPT Pro, which also thought it had, and hallucinated several references which, when I tracked them down, did not have this inequality in them. So it remains open whether this inequality is new or not. GPT also gave several false proofs of this inequality. I am sure that the proof given in your note is fine, but given the existence of subtle slips in other GPT attempts to prove it I'd feel more comfortable if someone could provide a Lean formalised version of this main inequality (or something equivalent).

The best currently known unconditional lower bound is\[ \rho(f)\ \gg\ \frac{1}{n\sqrt{\log n}}, \]proved in Theorem 8 of Krishnapur, Lundberg and Ramachandran, On the area of polynomial lemniscates (2025). (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
